segments.s742-g113: چو (when) + past verb بشنید (he heard) opens a temporal subordinate clause; رامشگر is the subject (the peacemaker) and آواز اوی is the object phrase 'his voice' where اوی is the possessive 'his'. Word order is Classical Persian: temporal conjunction + verb + subject + object.
segments.s742-g114: This is a verbless predicate clause (copula omitted, common in older Persian): همان (that very) modifies خوب‌گفتار (well‑spoken/eloquent) which modifies دمساز (accompanist/musician); اوی here again functions as a possessive ('his' / 'of him'), linking the accompanist to the previously mentioned person. The clause asserts identity/role rather than giving a new action.
segments.s742-g115: فرود آمد is a past‑tense verbal phrase 'he alighted/came down'; از + noun (از شاخ سرو سهی) is the usual prepositional phrase 'from the bough of the dark cypress' (شاخ = bough, سرو = cypress, سهی = a poetic epithet 'dark'/'black'). The verb precedes the source phrase, a common Classical ordering.
segments.s742-g116: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/progressive past form ('was going' / 'went on'), with the old prefix همی marking continuous action. با introduces accompaniment ('with'), here abstract qualities رامش (calm/solace) and فرهی (nobility/cultured dignity) joined by و 'and' to describe the manner of his going.
segments.s742-g117: Two successive past actions: بیامد 'he came' followed by بمالید 'he rubbed' (both narrative past). برخاک روی = 'his face upon the ground' where برخاک is the prepositional phrase 'on(to) the earth' and روی (face) is the direct object; Classical Persian allows this compact sequencing of verbs and their objects.
segments.s742-g118: بدو گفت is the indirect‑speech construction 'to him said'; here گفت is the reporting verb 'said' and what follows is the reported speech. خسرو is the speaker's name (Khosrow). چه مردی بگوی is an elliptical command/request in old Persian order: literally 'what man (are) — say!' i.e. 'tell (me) what kind of man you are.' Note: the provided sense list for گفت and بگوی does not include the verbal senses they bear here.
segments.s742-g119: بدو گفت again 'to him said' (introducing direct speech). شاهایکی is a vocative 'O King!' (شاها + vocative particle ی), and بنده‌ام is the speaker's humble self‑designation 'I am your servant' (بنده = servant with the 1st‑person enclitic). Word order places the indirect object + reporting verb before the quoted address and statement.
segments.s742-g120: به آواز تو is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/through your voice' or 'at your call' (به functioning as 'by'/'through'); در جهان زنده‌ام is the predicate 'I am alive in the world' where زنده‌ + ام (enclitic) = 'I am alive.' The clause states dependence on the addressee's voice for the speaker's life/status.
segments.s742-g121: سراسر (adverb) precedes the verb to emphasize completeness ('throughout/entirely'); بگفت is the past narrative 'he told/recounted'; آنچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which/whatever'; بود is the past copula 'was' describing the state of that thing; از here functions as 'of/from' linking the content to بنه; بنه is a noun 'bundle/heap' (poetic object) indicating what contained the things recounted.
segments.s742-g122: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; رفت is the past verb 'went/entered'; اندر is the preposition 'in/within'; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; دل is the noun 'heart' (emotional center); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; یک‌تنه is an adjectival phrase 'one-bodied / single-bodied' (literary: 'single body' or 'alone') that completes the description 'one heart and one body.'
segments.s742-g123: بدیدار here functions adverbially/constructively 'at the meeting/at the sight of'; او is the third-person pronoun 'him'; شاد is the predicate adjective 'joyful'; شد is the past inchoative 'became'; شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s742-g124: بسان is a comparative preposition 'like/as'; گلستان is the simile's noun 'rose-garden'; به is a preposition introducing the spatial/figurative complement (here rendered 'beneath / in relation to'); ماه is 'moon' used poetically as an emblem of beauty; بهار is 'spring' specifying which moon is meant—'the spring moon.' The phrase is a compact simile: 'like a rose-garden at the spring moon.'
segments.s742-g125: به introduces the addressee 'to/for'; سرکش is a vocative/epithet 'the headstrong/insolent one' being addressed; چنین precedes گفت to mark 'thus' (introducing reported speech); گفت here is the narrative verb 'said' (poetic usage); کای is the vocative particle 'O'; بد is an attributive 'bad/ill' modifying هنر; هنر is 'skill/art' so the phrase 'کای بد هنر' addresses someone as 'O poorly-skilled one.'
segments.s742-g126: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you' addressing the addressee; چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; حنظلی (from حنظل) evokes the bitter colocynth (a bitter plant) used metaphorically to say 'you are bitter'; باربد is a proper name (Barbad) used as the counterpart in the comparison; the second چون again marks the simile 'as/like'; شکر is 'sugar' — the contrast sets the addressee as bitter and Barbad as sweet.
segments.s742-g127: چرا is the interrogative 'why'; دور کردی is the past tense verb complex 'you made [him] distant/kept [him] away' (کردی = you did + دور = away/remote); تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you' (subject); او is the object 'him'; را is the definite/object marker attached to او; ز is the preposition 'from'; من is the oblique pronoun 'me' as the object of ز ('from me').
segments.s742-g128: دریغ is a noun meaning 'pity/reluctance' and here names the feeling; آمدت is a light-verb idiom meaning 'occurred to you / befell you' (literally 'it came to you') forming the predicate 'did [pity/reluctance] come to you'; او is 'him' (the person in question); درین is the fused prepositional phrase 'در این' ('in this') where ین is the orthographic suffix representing این; انجمن is 'assembly/gathering' — together yielding 'Did you feel reluctant about him in this assembly?'
segments.s742-g73: This clause is an inverted/poetic order: the prepositional phrase 'بدان نامداران' (to those renowned men) comes before the verb 'بفرمود' (commanded/said), and finally the subject/title 'شاه' (the king) appears. 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past verb meaning he ordered/said; the whole clause means 'the king commanded to those renowned men'.
segments.s742-g74: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause (that/so that). 'جویند' is a present subjunctive/indicative plural verb here meaning 'they search/seek'. 'سرتاسر' is an adverbial adjective meaning 'throughout' and modifies the following noun phrase 'آن جشنگاه' (that place/occasion of celebration). Together: 'that they search throughout that place of celebration.'
segments.s742-g75: 'فراوان' is an adverb modifying the verb 'بجستند' (past plural 'they searched/sought'), so the phrase means 'they searched abundantly/extensively.' The conjunction 'و' links this to 'باز آمدند' (they returned/they came back), with 'باز' here meaning 'again' or 'back.' Both verbs are past plural forms describing completed actions.
segments.s742-g76: The prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک' means 'toward/near to'; 'خسرو' here refers to the sovereign/king; 'فراز آمدند' is a compound verbal expression meaning 'came up/approached' (فراز = upward/on high used adverbially). Altogether: 'they approached near the king.'
segments.s742-g77: 'جهاندیده' is a substantive/adjectival epithet 'worldly‑wise' or 'experienced man' and functions as the agent. 'آنگه' = 'then' (temporal/conjunctive). 'ره اندر گرفت' literally 'took the way within' — an idiom meaning 'he took the floor' or 'he began (to speak/take up the matter)'; 'گرفت' is past tense 'took'. Word order is typical older Persian where the verbal phrase comes after the subject phrase.
segments.s742-g78: 'که' introduces what he said. 'از بخت شاه' = 'because/of the king's fortune' (از = from/of, بخت = fortune/fate, شاه = king). 'این نباشد شگفت' is a negative copular/substantive clause meaning 'this would not be (a) wonder' — i.e., 'this is not surprising.' The verb 'نباشد' is an older/poetic negative present/subjunctive form of 'to be.'
segments.s742-g79: 'که' here introduces a wish or outcome. 'گردد' (subjunctive/optative sense 'may become' or 'become') takes the following noun phrase as predicate: 'گل سبز' ('green flower') — a poetic image — and 'رامشگرش' = 'his comforter/peacemaker' (رامشگر + possessive -ش). The clause expresses a wish/blessing that the green flower become his solace.
segments.s742-g80: Another optative/blessing: 'که' introduces it, 'جاوید' = 'eternal', 'بادا' is an optative particle 'may it be', and the two nouns 'سر و افسرش' ('his head and his helmet') are the objects of the blessing. Word order is poetic; 'بادا' marks the wish and applies to the preceding adjective.
segments.s742-g57: This is a short prepositional + verb phrase: ز (from) + ایوان (iwan/porch) + بیامد (came) followed by the compound preposition ب + دان (here functioning as 'to that' in the older poetic form) and the object جشنگاه (festive place). Word order is verb after the source phrase (poetic flexibility). Note that بدَان is the fused form meaning 'to that' (b + dān), so the preposition b governs the following demonstrative/old form.
segments.s742-g58: بیاراست is a past/transitive verbal form 'adorned' (verb) appearing before its subject (poetic V–S order); پیروزگر is the agent 'the victor'. جای شاه is the direct object 'the king's place/seat'. The clause reads 'the victor adorned the king's place.'
segments.s742-g59: بیامد is the past tense 'came' placed before the nominal subject (poetic inversion). پری چهره is a compound adjective-noun 'fairy-faced' or 'beautiful of face' modifying میگسار (cupbearer), so the whole nominal phrase means 'a fair-faced cupbearer.'
segments.s742-g60: This gives what she carried: یکی جام 'one/a certain cup' with two prepositional phrases: بر کف 'on (the) palm' (بر = on/upon + کف = palm) and بر شهریار 'to/toward the king' (here بر = toward). The repeated بر marks location ('on the palm') then direction/recipient ('to the king').
segments.s742-g61: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign.' بستد is a past verb 'shut/off/withheld' (here 'withheld' or 'stopped'); ز introduces the source or object 'from' (ز کودک نبید = 'the child's wine' or 'wine from the child' i.e. wine given to the child). The clause thus means the sovereign stopped/withheld the child's wine.
segments.s742-g62: بلور (crystal/glass) is the subject. از marks cause or origin ('from' the wine), می‌سرخ (literally present-stem 'redden') combined with شد 'became' yields a resultative 'became/reddened,' and ناپدید 'vanished' is a predicate adjective/adverbial result. Grammatically this describes the glass reddening because of the wine and then disappearing.
segments.s742-g63: بدانگه که is a temporal/discourse phrase 'at that moment when.' خورشید برگشت is 'the sun turned/returned' and the adjective زرد here means 'pale/wan' (poetically describing the sun's hue at setting). The clause is a temporal subordinate clause setting the time.
segments.s742-g64: همی‌بود is an imperfect/past-continuous sense 'it was/continued to be.' تاگشت introduces the endpoint 'until (it) turned' and governs the following noun phrase شب لاژورد 'the night (became) lapis‑lazuli/azure.' Together: 'it remained so until the night turned ultramarine.'
segments.s742-g41: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/when'); تاچون here means 'when' (a temporal marker). بود is the past copula ('was'). شاه را is the king marked with را as definite direct/topic marker ('the king'). جشنگاه is the noun 'place/occasion of celebration' and is the complement of 'was'. Together: 'that when it was the king's festival'.
segments.s742-g42: ببینم is the (classical) present/subjunctive-like form 'I may see' or 'that I might see' (purpose/optative nuance). نهفته is an adjective 'hidden'. یکی is indefinite 'one/a certain' (a single instance). روی شاه = 'the face/aspect of the king' (روی = face/aspect). The verb precedes its complements in this poetic order, so: 'I might see a certain hidden face of the king.'
segments.s742-g43: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (he said to him). گفت here is verbal 'said' (reported speech marker). مردوی is 'the man' (a dialectal/poetic form). کایدون is a vocative or direct address/nickname 'schemer' or 'treacherous one' used in speech. کن م is the first-person present/future 'I do/I will do' (introducing the speaker's action).
segments.s742-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. مغز تو places the possession after the noun ('the brain of you' = 'your brain'). اندیشه is the direct object 'thought/intention'. بیرون کنم is a compound verb phrase 'bring out / take out' with کنم 'I will do' (first-person), so 'I will take the thought out of your brain.' Word order is noun+possessor then object then verb, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s742-g45: چو = 'when/as'. خسرو is the proper name Khosrow. همی‌خواست is an archaic imperfect/intentional 'was wanting/was about to'. کاید here functions as 'plot/plan' (the object of his intention). بباغ is a prepositional phrase ('to/in the garden' — ب + باغ). The clause thus says Khosrow was on the point of his plot in the garden.
segments.s742-g46: دل میزبان is a genitival phrase 'the host's heart' (noun + possessor). شد is the past 'became'. چو here is a simile particle 'like/as'. روشن چراغ = 'a bright lamp' (روشن = bright, چراغ = lamp). The whole clause is a simile: 'the host's heart became like a bright lamp.'
segments.s742-g47: بر here means 'toward/to' (motion toward someone). باربد is the name Barbad. شد is 'rose/went' or 'he proceeded' (past of شدن used as intransitive 'went to'). بگفت is 'and he said' (reported-speech verb). آنک introduces the reported content 'that', and شاه is 'the king' (the subject of the reported clause). Together the segment sets up: 'He went to Barbad and said that the king...'.
segments.s742-g48: همی‌رفت is an imperfect/past progressive 'was going / was about to go'. خواهد is an archaic future/intentional auxiliary 'will/wants to' used to report the king's intention. بران is a deictic/adverbial 'to that place / thither' and جشنگاه is 'the festival/celebration place'. Together: 'that the king was about to / intended to go to that festival.'
segments.s742-g17: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو is the dative/enclitic preposition “to him,” and گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” رامشگری is a verbal-noun (masdar) meaning “peacemaking/conciliation” or “being conciliatory,” and بر درست functions as a predicate meaning “is proper/right” (literally ‘upon/right’), so the whole segment says something like “he said to him that being conciliatory is right.”
segments.s742-g18: که introduces an explanation clause (“that / for”). از ... برترست is the comparative construction: از marks the standard of comparison (‘than’/‘from’), من is the first‑person object ‘me,’ and هنربرترست (= هنر برتر است) is the verbless comparative ‘is superior in skill.’ به سال و هنر here means ‘in respect of age and art/skill’ (به + nouns = ‘with regard to / in’).
segments.s742-g19: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning ‘it must not / it should not be that…’. که introduces the subordinate clause, and در پیش خسرو means ‘in the presence of the king’ (در + پیش = ‘before/in the presence of’). شود is the present/subjunctive/potential form ‘becomes / happens’—here used impersonally with نباید.
segments.s742-g20: This clause gives the content of the warning: که = ‘that’; ما گشتیم (ما کهنه گشتیم) ‘we become old/are made old’—گشتیم is past/pluperfective of شدن used as ‘become’; و links the two halves; او نو شود ‘he becomes new’ (شود again indicating becoming). The construction contrasts two states by means of گشتیم / شود.
segments.s742-g21: ز here is the poetic preposition often glossed ‘about/from’ (introducing the thing heard about). چو + past (چو بشنید) = ‘when he heard.’ دربان شاه is a genitive phrase ‘the king’s gatekeeper’ (دربان = gatekeeper, شاه = king); word order is typical of poetry where the circumstance precedes the verb.
segments.s742-g22: ز رامشگر ساده is the object/target of the action introduced by the verb that follows: رامشگر ساده = ‘the simple/naive peacemaker.’ بربست is the past-tense verb ‘he closed/sealed/blocked’ (here ‘barred’), and راه = ‘the way/path’; so the verb phrase means ‘he barred the way against the simple peacemaker.’
segments.s742-g23: چو + past (چو رفتی) = ‘when he went / when he came’; in classical poetic usage رفتی can function as a 3rd‑person past. به نزدیک او = ‘to his near presence / close to him’ (به + نزدیک = ‘toward, near to’), and باربد is the proper name Barbad placed at the end as subject.
segments.s742-g24: همش کار بد بود is a simple descriptive clause: همش ‘all of it/everything,’ کار ‘work/performance,’ بد ‘bad,’ بود = past ‘was.’ و هم بار بد repeats the judgment for بار (here ‘bar’ most likely meaning ‘tune / burden / load’ in context of music): هم = ‘also,’ بار = ‘load/tune,’ بد = ‘bad.’ Together: ‘all his work was bad and his tune was bad too.’
segments.s742-g9: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). The clause follows classical word order where the past verb بشنید ('he heard') appears before its subject مرد ('the man') and the demonstrative آن ('that') points to what was heard. بجوشیدش is composed of بجوشید ('boiled') plus the enclitic -ش ('in/for him'), so the phrase means 'it boiled within him' with آز (greed) as the thing that boiled.
segments.s742-g10: وگرچه is a concessive 'although'. نبودش is نبود + enclitic -ش = 'there was not for him' (i.e., 'he did not have'); the prepositional phrase به چیزی نیاز ('need for anything') follows: چیزی here functions as 'anything' in a negated context and نیاز is the noun 'need'. Together the clause means 'although he was in no need of anything.'
segments.s742-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کشور is the realm/country. بشد here is an archaic past-form meaning 'he went/left' (classical usage). تا in this sequence functions as 'as far as/until' and combines with the destination phrase به درگاه شاه ('to the king's court'), giving a motion clause 'he went from the country as far as the king's court.'
segments.s742-g12: همی‌کرد is an imperfective past habitual ('was doing' / 'would do'), here introducing his habitual action at the court. رامشگران را uses را as the definite-object marker ('the peacemakers' as object). نگاه here functions as the verbal noun used with کرد ('to look/watch'); together همی‌کرد ... نگاه = 'he would look/watch the peacemakers.' Note the classical tendency to place the object (رامشگران را) before the light verb construction (همی‌کرد نگاه).
segments.s742-g13: چو بشنید is 'when he heard' (temporal clause). سرکش modifies دل ('his rebellious/insolent heart') with the possessive enclitic ش on دل (دلش = 'his heart'). تیره شد is a simple past copular construction 'became dark/ominous.' The sequence means 'when he heard it, his rebellious heart grew dark.'
segments.s742-g14: به زخم سرود is a prepositional phrase meaning literally 'by/at the wound of the song' (a poetic way to say 'by the cutting power of the verse'). اندرو is an adverbial/inward locative 'in him' or 'within him'. خیره شد is a past resultative 'became transfixed/astonished' (خیره used predicatively). Together: 'he was transfixed by the song's wound within him.'
segments.s742-g15: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. به درگاه سالار is the destination/prepositional phrase 'to the court of the lord/commander'. بار here is a noun 'load' or 'parcel' that will be linked to the following light-verb clause (i.e., what he produced or set down when he arrived).
segments.s742-g16: درم کرد uses کرد as a light verb with درم ('dirham') as its object — literally 'he did dirhams,' i.e. 'he gave/handed over dirhams' (classical idiom). The clause continues with و ('and') coordinating دینار چندی نثار: دینار چندی means 'several/some dinars' (چندی is a quantifier after the noun), and نثار is 'to offer/dedicate' used here as the verb ('he offered several dinars').
segments.s742-g105: چنین گفت is a conventional narrative formula meaning “thus said” (چنین = 'thus/so' before the verb گفت = 'said'). کاین‌گر starts the reported clause: که + این‌گر (a poetic relative/demonstrative) = 'that this (one) ...'. فرشته is the noun 'angel' serving as predicate in the reported clause. بدی here is an archaic/poetic form of the copula ('was/is') in context (classical spelling varies), linking فرشته to the following description; the printed lemma senses given do not include the verbal use, so the sense index is left as -1.
segments.s742-g106: ز ... و زعنبر is a serial prepositional phrase 'from/of musk and ambergris' (ز = poetic 'from/of', مشک = 'musk', و = 'and', زعنبر = 'ambergris'). سرشته is a passive/adjectival participle meaning 'fashioned/created' (used predicatively). The final بدی again functions as the copula/past verbal form in this poetic line rather than the noun sense listed.
segments.s742-g107: وگر = 'if' (introduces the conditional). دیو بودی is a past conditional/contrary-to-fact 'had been a demon' (دیو = 'demon', بودی = 2nd-person past 'you were' / 'you had been'). نگفتی here is the verbal clause 'you would not have said/sung' in the conditional sense; the provided noun senses do not match this verbal use so its sense index is -1. سرود is the noun 'song/ode' (object of گفتن/سرودن).
segments.s742-g108: همان نیز is 'likewise/that also' (همان = 'the same/that', نیز = 'also'). نشناختی is a verbal past-negative/conditional sense 'you would not / did not recognize/know' (here used as the continuation of the conditional), but the listed lemma senses are nominal so the index is -1. زخم رود is a poetic compound: زخم ('pluck/strike' poetically as 'wound') + رود = 'string instrument' (رود here = lute/string instrument), i.e. 'the lute's plucking/strike'.
segments.s742-g109: بجویید درباغ is an imperative 'search in the garden' (بجویید = 2nd-person plural/formal 'search', درباغ = 'in the garden'). تا این کجاست is a purposive/relative clause 'to find/see where this is' (تا = 'so that / in order that', این = 'this', کجاست = 'where (it) is' with the copula -ست).
segments.s742-g110: همه باغ و گلشن lists the places 'the whole garden and the flower-grove' (همه = 'all/entire', باغ = 'garden', و = 'and', گلشن = 'flower-garden'). چپ و دست راست gives location 'to the left and to the right' using two nouns/adverbs: چپ = 'left', دست here = 'side/hand' and راست = 'right' (راست functions directionally 'to the right'). Word order is typical Persian: general locus first, then directional modifiers.
segments.s742-g111: دهان و برش پر ز گوهر کنم is a declarative first-person statement: دهان = 'mouth', و = 'and', برش here refers to 'countenance/feature' (a noun meaning 'section/profile' used poetically for face), پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', گوهر = 'gem(s)/jewels', کنم = 'I will make/do' (1st-person present-future sense 'I will fill'). Word order places the objects before the verb كنم, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s742-g112: برین رود سازانش مهتر کنم: بر = 'on/upon', ین is the orthographic suffix of این (here 'this') so برین = 'upon this'. رود in this musical context is best read as 'string instrument / lute'. سازانش = 'its musicians/players' (سازان + possessive ش = 'its players'), مهتر کنم = 'I will make (them) chief' (مهتر = 'chief', کنم = 'I will make/do'). The clause therefore says the speaker will raise the instrument's players to chief status.
segments.s742-g129: به آواز او is an adverbial phrase 'at/at the sound of his voice' (به + noun + possessive او). شاد is used adverbially to indicate manner ('joyfully'). می‌درکشید is an imperfective/past-continuous verb (prefix می‑ plus past-stem) meaning 'was drawing in / drew in' (here 'drew a breath' or 'inhaled'), so the whole segment says that on his voice she joyfully drew in (a breath).
segments.s742-g130: همان 'that very' or 'the same' foregrounds the object; جام یاقوت is a noun phrase 'ruby cup' with the gem-name yāqūt post‑modifying جام. بر + سرکشید here functions as a classical compound meaning 'to raise to the head and drink / to drink up' (object-marker را is omitted, a poetic omission). سرکشید is a simple past form describing the action of lifting the cup and drinking.
segments.s742-g131: برین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این/ین + گونه). تا 'until' introduces the limit, and سرسوی خواب literally 'toward sleep' (سرسوی = toward/the direction of; خواب = sleep). کرد is the past-form of کردن used here to mark the action (in classical Persian compounds like خواب کرد = 'fell asleep' or 'turned to sleep').
segments.s742-g132: دهانش 'her mouth' (noun + possessive suffix). پر از = 'full of' (پر functioning as adjective 'full'). در خوشاب is a poetic genitive/phrase meaning 'of pleasant/sweet drink' (در here marks the internal/in‑something relation), and کرد again is 'made / caused' (i.e., 'filled her mouth with'). The clause means she filled her mouth with sweet drink.
segments.s742-g133: بِبد is an archaic/past copular form meaning 'it was / there was' (classical poetic past). باربد is the proper name. شاه رامشگران is an honorific apposition 'the king/chief of the comforters' (شاه used as 'chief' and رامشگران 'comforters/minstrels'). The segment identifies Barbad with this title.
segments.s742-g134: یکی here functions as 'one / someone / a certain one' (introducing an individual). نامدارای is an adjectival/noun form meaning 'renown / famed' ('famous'). شد is the past of شدن 'became'. از مهتران literally 'from/among the chiefs/elders' (از used in the sense 'of, among'); مهتران is plural 'chiefs/elders' (here meaning leading men).
segments.s742-g135: سر آمد is a fixed expression 'the head/top came' used idiomatically to mean 'has come to an end / is finished'. کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. قصه باربد is the noun phrase 'the story/tale of Barbad' (قصه = tale, باربد = name). Together: 'Now the story of Barbad has ended.'
segments.s742-g136: مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be / lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'. باشد is subjunctive/present form of بودن 'be' here expressing a wish or fear. تو را places 'you' as the indirect/object (the rā here emphasizes the object). یار بد is 'an evil/bad companion' (یار = companion, بد = bad). The clause is a conventional wish: 'May it not be that a bad companion befall you.'
segments.s742-g81: بیاورد is a literary/past third‑person verb meaning 'brought' or 'caused to bring' and appears at the clause start (verb‑initial order common in epic Persian). جامی دگر is the object phrase 'another cup' (جامی = a cup, with دگر = 'another' following it). میگسار (cupbearer) follows the object and functions as the agent/subject in this line, a typical poetic word order (Verb — Object — Subject).
segments.s742-g82: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از + خوب رخ = 'from the fair face' (از = 'from/of', خوب = 'beautiful/fair', رخ = 'face/countenance'). بستد is a literary past form here meaning 'turned away/closed (one's face)' — 'رخ بستن' = to turn one's face away — and آن شهریار is 'that king' (demonstrative + title). Word order is standard: temporal conjunction + prepositional phrase + verb + appositive subject.
segments.s742-g83: زننده is an agent noun 'the striker/player' (one who strikes the strings). دگرگون is an adjective 'changed/altered' modifying the action or the instrument. بیاراست is a literary past/perfective verb 'adorned/adjusted/tuned' (here used transitively), and رود in this poetic context is the stringed instrument (rud, i.e. lute/lyre). The clause thus reads: 'the player changed/tuned the instrument.'
segments.s742-g84: برآورد is a past third‑person verb 'brought forth/produced' (here 'set about/uttered'); ناگاه is the adverb 'suddenly'; دیگر modifies سرود as 'another/a different' song; سرود is 'song/ode'. The verb precedes the adverb and object in this poetic line: 'he suddenly brought forth another song.'
segments.s742-g85: که is the relative 'which/that' introducing a clause. پیکار = 'battle' and گردش = 'turn/round/circuit' (here idiomatically 'in the course/turns of battle'); همی‌خواندند is the imperfective/past habitual 'they used to sing/chant'. Together the phrase means 'which they used to chant during the rounds of battle.'
segments.s742-g86: چنین modifies نام: 'such a name' or 'such an epithet'. ز is the preposition 'from', آواز او = 'his voice' (آواز = voice/song, او = he/his). راندند is a past plural verb 'they sent/they cast/they uttered' (poetically 'they issued/drove forth'), so the clause literally says 'from his voice they sent forth such a name' — i.e., they cried out such an epithet with his voice.
segments.s742-g87: چو is 'when'. آن دانشی is 'that learned one' (دانشی here used substantively as 'the learned man' or 'man of knowledge'). گفت is the past verb 'spoke/said' (classical past). و connects clauses, and خسرو شنید = 'Khosrow heard' (خسرو subject, شنید past 'heard'). The sequence is temporal: 'When that learned man spoke and Khosrow heard...'.
segments.s742-g88: به is the preposition 'at/to/by'; آواز او = 'his voice' (آواز = voice/song, او = his). جام is the object 'the cup'. می‌در کشید is a poetic verbal phrase whose core is کشید (past 'drew/dragged/lifted'); می‌در here is an archaic/poetic element attached to the verb, so the whole means 'he drew/lifted the cup' or 'he took up the cup.' Word order: prepositional phrase (by his voice) + object + verb phrase.
segments.s742-g97: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. دگر باره is an adverbial phrase ('again, once more') with دگر 'another/again' + باره 'occasion/instance'. بانگ سرود is a noun phrase 'the cry/voice of the song' (بانگ 'sound/cry' modifies سرود 'song'). Word order places the verb before the adjunct phrase as is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s742-g98: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/that same'. ساخته کرده is a compound participial/perfect-like expression: ساخته 'made' + کرده (here functioning as part of a verbal/adjectival compound) giving 'made/contrived' and modifying آواز. آواز رود is a tight nominal phrase where آواز 'voice' is qualified by رود; here رود is best read as the noun 'rūd' (string instrument), so 'the voice of the instrument' — a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s742-g99: همی here marks habitual/continuous action ('still/continually'). سبز در سبز is a quoted or reiterated phrase literally 'green in green' (the repeated adjective is the sung phrase). خوانی is 2nd person singular present 'you sing/recite' (the verb follows the phrase it governs), and کنون is the adverb 'now'.
segments.s742-g100: برین گونه is an archaic compound meaning 'in this manner' (بر + ـین forming 'on/over/with this kind/way'). سازند is 3rd person plural present 'they make/compose' used here to state what is done in general, and مکر و فسون are the coordinated nouns 'deceit and enchantment' (objects of سازند).
segments.s742-g101: چوبشنید is a fused temporal clause (چو + بشنید) meaning 'when he heard'. پرویز is the subject name. برپای خاست is an idiomatic expression 'rose to his feet' (برپای 'to/onto his feet' + خاست past 'arose'), with the locative/adverbial phrase preceding the verb.
segments.s742-g102: به آواز او is a prepositional phrase 'at/to his voice' (meaning 'on his call' or 'at his signal'); او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. بر یکی جام خواست uses بر before an indefinite object (یکی جام 'a cup') and خواست is the past tense 'he wanted/asked for' — together 'he asked for a cup'.
segments.s742-g103: که here introduces a clause 'for/that' and بود is the past copula 'there was'. اندر آن جام is a locative phrase 'in that cup'. یک من نبید reads as a quantity: یک من is a unit-of-measure 'one man' (i.e., a measure of weight/quantity) and نبید is 'wine'. Note: the lexicon provided lists من only as the pronoun 'I', but here it is the weight/measure, so that sense is not present in the supplied senses.
segments.s742-g104: به یکدم is an adverbial phrase 'in one moment' or 'in a single instant'. می‌روشن is a present-form verb meaning 'they shine / they become bright' (used poetically); اندر کشید here uses کشید (past of کشیدن) in the sense 'drew in / drank down', a verbal use not listed in the supplied senses, so that sense is marked as unavailable. The clause as a whole describes the rapid action of taking the wine in at once.
segments.s742-g89: بفرمود is a past, honorific verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’ (3rd person). The clause that follows is an embedded command: کاین (this) is the demonstrative object and رابجای is a fused sequence of را + بجای (‘to/into its place’); آورید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive ‘bring/put’. Word order is typical classical Persian: the verb (آورید) comes at the end and the object is marked by را before the locative بجای.
segments.s742-g90: همه باغ = ‘the whole garden’ (همه as ‘entire’, باغ = garden). یک سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase here meaning ‘throughout/utterly’ (سر used in the sense of ‘top/entirety’), and به پای آورید literally ‘bring to the foot’ but functions idiomatically as a command ‘set/arrange/prepare’. آورید is again the 2nd‑person plural imperative; the sequence commands that the entire garden be arranged from top to bottom.
segments.s742-g91: بجستند is past 3rd person plural and here means ‘they sprang/leapt’ (movement, not ‘seek’). بسیار modifies the verb as ‘in great numbers/with much haste’, and هر سوی باغ is a directional phrase ‘to every side of the garden’ (هر = each/every, سوی = toward/side, باغ = garden).
segments.s742-g92: ببردند is past 3rd person plural ‘they carried/took’. The phrase زیر درختان چراغ places the object and location: زیر = under/beneath, درختان = trees (plural), چراغ = lamps/light — so they carried lamps to/and placed them under the trees. The verb is transitive and appears after the locative phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s742-g93: ندیدند is the negative past (they did not see). چيزى جز is an idiomatic negation ‘nothing except/other than’; جز = except, and از + nouns (بید و سرو) marks the items excepted: willow and cypress. The clause therefore means ‘they saw nothing except willow and cypress.’
segments.s742-g94: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning ‘stately’ or ‘with a proud gait’, modifying the implied actors. به زیر گل = ‘beneath the flowers’ (به marking direction/toward + زیر = under + گل = flower), اندر is a locative/prepositional particle ‘in/among’, and تذرو is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning ‘they scatter/they strew’ (here a present or narrative poetic form). Together: ‘stately, they scattered (flowers) under/among the blooms.’
segments.s742-g95: شهنشاه is the subject ‘the king/emperor’. پس is a temporal adverb ‘then/afterwards’. جام دیگر = ‘another cup’ (جام = cup, دیگر = another). بخواست is past 3rd person singular ‘he desired/asked for’ (classical simple past), so the whole clause reads ‘then the king asked for another cup.’
segments.s742-g96: بر آواز is a prepositional phrase ‘at the sound/at the call’ (بر = on/at, آواز = voice/sound). سربرآورد is a compound classical verb meaning ‘he raised his head’ or more generally ‘he appeared/lifted his head’ (past 3rd person). راست here is an adverb meaning ‘straight/straightaway’ and modifies سربرآورد to indicate immediacy or directness.
segments.s742-g25: ندادی is the past negative of دادن (you did not give); the verb appears first here (typical of Persian poetry) with ورا (ورا = او را) immediately after as the indirect object ‘to him.’ بار سالار بار is a doubled-noun phrase: بار = burden/office and سالار = commander/lord, so the phrase means ‘the burden/office of the commander.’ The second بار repeats/echoes بار for poetic emphasis (‘the burden, the burden of the lord’).
segments.s742-g26: نه نیزش begins the negation continuation: نه = nor, نیزش = نیز + ‑ش (also to him). بدی here is a noun that in the lexicon is listed as ‘evil/harm’; in this line the poet uses it in a general sense of ‘a (bad) thing’ or ‘any (undesirable) grant’ — the phrase is elliptical. مردمی خواستار literally ‘desired/by-the-people’ (مردمی = of the people; خواستار = desirous/what is asked for), so the clause says that he did not receive even what the people asked for. Word order is compressed/elliptical as typical in epic verse.
segments.s742-g27: چو here means ‘when’ (temporal subordinating conjunction). نومید is an adjective/noun meaning ‘hopeless/despairing (one)’ describing the subject; برگشت is the past tense ‘returned’; زان = از + آن = ‘from that’; بارگاه = ‘palace/court.’ The clause therefore reads ‘when (he), despairing, returned from that court.’
segments.s742-g28: ابا بربط آمد is a compressed poetic expression meaning ‘he came with the barbat (lute).’ In prose one would expect با بربط آمد (‘came with a lute’); here the form is slightly contracted/poetic. آمد = ‘came’; سوی = ‘toward/to’; باغ شاه = ‘the king’s garden.’ The whole segment gives the motion toward the king’s garden, with the instrument (barbat) attached to the subject.
segments.s742-g29: کجا here functions as a locative/introductory ‘where’ or ‘there where’; باغبان = ‘gardener’; بود = past of بودن ‘was’ (here simple past ‘there was’); مردوی is a proper name (Marduy) formed from مردو; نام = ‘named’ or ‘called.’ Word order places the location/identification before the verb as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s742-g30: شد = ‘became’; از دیدنش = از + دیدن + ‑ش (‘from/at his seeing him’ = ‘on seeing him’), where the enclitic ‑ش = ‘him’; باربد is the proper name Barbad; شادکام = ‘joyful/fortunate.’ The clause literally: ‘Barbad became joyful from seeing him.’ Note the causal/temporal use of از + infinitive/masdar construction common in older Persian.
segments.s742-g31: بدان باغ is the demonstrative phrase ‘to that garden’ (ب + دان forming بدان). رفتی is a past tense form ‘went’ (poetic ending -ی can appear where modern prose expects رفت); به نوروز شاه = ‘for/at the king’s Nowruz’ (به نوروز = at Nowruz, شاه = the king). Word order is compressed: the temporal/occasion (Nowruz) and the actor (شاه) follow the verb phrase.
segments.s742-g32: دو هفته ببودی = ‘you/he were/would be two weeks’ — ببودی is an archaic/poetic past-form ending that can read as ‘he stayed’ in context (the subject is Barbad or the king’s retinue). بدان جشنگاه = ‘in that place of celebration’ (بدان = in/at that; جشنگاه = festal place). Together: ‘(he) remained there for two weeks at that festive place.’
segments.s742-g33: نزد + شد here describes motion/arrival into someone's presence: 'نزد' = 'in the presence of / before'. The clause literally puts an adverb/adjective before the name (سبک باربد) then the prepositional phrase (نزد مردوی) and concludes with شد, used here as a past/perfective marker meaning ‘came/arrived’ or ‘happened’ in poetic register. Word order: Subject/epithet → prepositional phrase → verbal predicate.
segments.s742-g34: هم آن روز is an emphatic time phrase 'that very day' (هم as emphatic 'even/precisely'), با مرد marks the person he became close to, and همبوی شد is a copular/result clause 'became like‑scented / congenial'. شد is the past copula 'became'. Overall: 'On that same day (هم آن روز) he became congenial with the man.'
segments.s742-g35: چنین گفت is a fixed formula 'thus he said'. The verb phrase precedes the indirect object here: با باغبان = 'to/with the gardener' and باربد at the end identifies the speaker (subject). In poetry subject/object ordering can be inverted for emphasis: 'Thus said Barbad to the gardener.'
segments.s742-g36: که introduces reported speech ('that'/'saying that'); گویی functions idiomatically as 'as if' or 'it is as though' (a concessive/simile marker). The verbless predication 'تو جانی و من کالبد' uses verbless nominal predicates: 'you [are] the soul and I [am] the body'—the copula is omitted, a common poetic feature.
segments.s742-g37: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. آرزو خواهم is a light-object construction where خواهم (from خواستن) expresses desire/will in the first person: 'I wish/ I will ask'. از تو marks the source/recipient 'of you', and یکی is the object 'one [thing]/a certain thing'—'I now wish/ask one thing of you.'
segments.s742-g38: کجاهست is an interrogative compound meaning 'is there/where is there?' (literally 'where-is'). نزدیک تو is a locative phrase 'near you' and اندکی is a quantifier 'a little / somewhat'. The overall sense is a question about whether there exists some place a little close to you: 'Is there somewhere a little near you?'
segments.s742-g39: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. آید is the present/subjunctive stem 'comes'. بدین is the fused preposition + demonstrative 'to this' (marked in the edition as ب + دین, i.e. به + این), followed by باغ 'garden' and شاه جهان 'king of the world' as the subject/object of the coming. Note: the tokenization in the data splits بدین unnaturally; grammatically treat it as 'به + این'.
segments.s742-g40: مرا راه ده is an imperative/request: 'give me a way/path' where مرا is the accusative 'me' (tokenized here as م + را) and راه ده is 'give [a] way' (ده imperative of دادن). تا introduces purpose 'so that' and ببینم is the subjunctive 'that I may see'; نهان modifies the verb meaning 'secretly / hidden'. The clause as a whole: 'give me a path so that I may see (him) secretly.'
segments.s742-g49: همه جامه را is a direct-object phrase: همه (quantifier 'all') modifies جامه ('garments') and را is the Persian definite/object marker attached after the noun. باربد سبز کرد is a past-tense clause with subject باربد (Barbad) and verbal compound سبز کرد where سبز is an adjective used as a resultative complement and کرد is the simple past 'made/did' (i.e. 'made green'). Word order is Subject + Complement + Past-verb (typical Persian), with the object phrase fronted.
segments.s742-g50: This short clause is an elliptic, verbless continuation: همان ('likewise / the same') links back to the previous action, followed by two instruments joined by و. The list 'بربط و رود' names two instruments; the following nouns 'ننگ و نبرد' are thematically linked (elliptically: songs/tones concerning 'nang' and 'nabard'). The clause relies on the previous verb ('made' / 'played') and therefore omits an explicit verb.
segments.s742-g51: بشد is a narrative/perfective verb here ('it happened / it came to pass') and introduces a clause of degree. تابجایی ('to such an extent' — تاب + جایی fused) indicates the limit/extent and که introduces the content clause 'that you became king'. خسرو is used as the title 'king' and شدی is the past second-person 'you became'. The structure is 'بشد + تا/تابجایی + که + subject + شدی' — 'it came to such a point that...'.
segments.s742-g52: This clause is compact and somewhat elliptical: بهاران 'spring' with نشستن (infinitive/gerund 'sitting / to sit') functions as a temporal-locative phrase ('sitting in/at the spring'); گهی is an adverb 'at times / sometimes'; نو شدی is a past/perfective predicate 'you became new' (شدی = 'you became' with نو as predicative adjective). The verb‌شُدن (شدی) carries the action; نشستن acts as a verbal noun indicating the occasion or setting.
segments.s742-g53: یکی introduces an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain / one'. سرو is the noun 'cypress'. بد here is an adjectival/qualifying element ('ill / poorly' or 'badly') modifying سبز ('green'), so the cluster سرو بد سبز is a compact descriptive phrase. The conjunction و links this description to برگش گشن, where برگش is a noun plus the enclitic -ش ('its leaves' in ordinary Persian usage) and گشن is a predicate/adjective 'hungry'. Note: برگش is written fused here with the possessive enclitic -ش.
segments.s742-g54: ورا is a directional/locative pronoun 'to it / toward it' placed before the noun شاخ ('branch'). چون is the comparative/simile 'like/as'. رزمگاه is the simile's noun 'battlefield', and پشن is a dialectal/poetic word 'back' (i.e. 'the back of the battlefield'). The whole phrase is a simile: 'its branch, like the back of a battlefield.'
segments.s742-g55: بران is an adverbial/temporal 'upon that / then' introducing the event. The clause shows a poetic/inverted order: سرو (cypress) and بربط (barbat) are placed close with شد ('became / came to be') used narratively — here indicating the appearance/coming together of the barbat beside the cypress. اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at the side / beside'. The result is a compact narrative: 'then the barbat came to be beside the cypress' (poetic word order).
segments.s742-g56: زمانی همی‌بود is an imperfective/past habitual expression: همی‌بود (همی + بود) conveys continued or repeated past action 'would be / remained for a while'. تا is the subordinator 'until' and is followed by شهریار ('the king'), here leaving the verb describing the king omitted (ellipsed) — a common poetic economy. The sense is 'they remained for some time until the king (came / arrived).'
segments.s742-g65: This clause names the agent and the action in poetic, slightly inverted order. زننده is an agent-noun ('the one who strikes/plays') placed before the locative phrase بران سرو ('by/on that cypress'); برداشت is the past tense verb 'took up' or 'lifted', and رود is the object 'lute' (a string instrument) placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s742-g66: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same (one)'; ساخته here is adjectival/predicative 'made/fashioned'; پهلوانی qualifies the following noun as 'heroic' or 'of heroism'; سرود is a noun 'song/ode'. Together they form a noun phrase 'that very song, made about heroism' (no separate finite verb in this short clause).
segments.s742-g67: یکی functions as an indefinite subject 'a certain/one'; نغز modifies دستان and here describes the manner of the hands ('dainty/skillful' hands); دستان is the instrument/accusative 'hands'; بزد is the past verb 'he struck'; بر درخت is the locative 'on/against the tree'. Word order places the manner before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s742-g68: کزان ('from which' / 'at which') links this clause to the previous action; خیره شد is a light verb construction meaning 'became/stood astonished' (خیره = amazed/staring, شد = became); مرد بیداربخت is the subject 'the ill-fated man'. The verb follows the predicative adjective, a common classical order.
segments.s742-g69: سرودی is a verbal noun/noun 'a song/ode' (object); به + آواز خوش is an adverbial phrase 'in a sweet/pleasant voice' (به here marks manner: 'by/with'); برکشید is the past tense 'he raised/let out' used of raising one's voice or beginning to sing. The clause therefore means 'he began/sang a song in a pleasant voice.'
segments.s742-g70: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding 'song' ('which'); اکنون = 'now'; تو = 'you'; خوانیش is a possessive form built on خوان (here referring to the act/instance of chanting/recital) — the exact classical nuance can be 'its recital/reading' rather than the literal 'banquet' sense of خوان; داد آفرید are two past verbs often used together for emphasis ('gave' / 'created' — i.e. 'have now provided / fashioned'). The clause thus says 'which you have now given and made.'
segments.s742-g71: بماندند is 3rd-person plural past 'they remained/they stayed'; یک مجلس is 'a assembly/gathering' and functions as the place/time of remaining; اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment' or 'in wonder' describing the state of that gathering. The phrase as a whole: 'they remained in the assembly in amazement.'
segments.s742-g72: همی is an adverb (here roughly 'then/at that time' or 'still'); هرکسی is the subject 'each person / everyone'; رای دیگر گرفت is the verb phrase 'took/assumed another opinion' (رای = opinion, گرفت = took). The clause means 'each person adopted a different opinion.'
segments.s742-g1: همی is a prefatory/adverbial particle giving a continuous or emphatic sense ('always/whenever'). هر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'every time/whenever'. شاه برتر is a nominal phrase 'the superior/overlord king' and گذشت is the past tense verb 'passed (by)'; the clause order is adverbial + subject + verb, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s742-g2: چوشد is a temporal conjunction 'when/as' introducing the time clause. سال شاهیش means 'the year of his kingship' (شاهی + possessive -ش), so شاهیش is a genitive/possessive form 'his kingship'. بر بیست و هشت is a numeric expression 'to/on twenty-eight' meaning 'reached the 28th (year)'; بر here marks the amount reached.
segments.s742-g3: کسی را نشد is an impersonal negative construction meaning 'no one was able / it did not happen to anyone' (کسی as an indefinite subject with را for emphasis). بر درش کار بد literally 'to/at his door a bad deed' places the action location ('بر در' = at the gate) and کار بد = 'a bad deed'. Note the past-negative نشد is used impersonally.
segments.s742-g4: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and درگاه here means 'the royal court / the presence (of the king)'. آگاه شد is a two-word verbal phrase 'became aware / learned' in the past (شد as full verb 'became' with predicate آگاه). باربد is the subject following the verb because of the inverted poetic word order.
segments.s742-g5: بدو is the indirect object/pronoun 'to him' (to that person). گفت is the verb 'said' (here presented as speech) and introduces reported speech. هر کس که شاه جهان begins a relative clause 'whoever the king of the world...' with هر کس که 'whoever/anyone who' governed by the following clause.
segments.s742-g6: گزیدست is an archaic/perfect form meaning 'has chosen' (گزیدن + -ست making a perfective/past sense). رامشگری functions as the noun object 'a conciliator/peacemaker' (an agentive/abstract noun), and در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' ('در' = in, نهان = hidden/secretly). Together they complete the relative clause begun in the previous segment.
segments.s742-g7: اگر is the conditional 'if'. با تو here is a comparative/instrumental phrase 'with you / alongside you' meaning 'in comparison to you' and او را برابر کند is the protasis 'he makes him equal (to you)' where را is the object marker and کند is the present/subjunctive-like verb 'makes/does' in a hypothetical sense.
segments.s742-g8: تو را is the object 'you' (marked by را). بر سر is a locative phrase 'upon the head', سرکش qualifies the people involved as 'the rebellious/headstrong ones', and افسر here means 'helmet' (a metonym for command). کند is the verb 'will make/place' applying to the whole clause; idiomatically the line says 'he will put the helmet on your head over the rebellious', i.e. appoint you commander of the rebels.

sentences.s742-s57: When the peacemaker heard his voice, he turned out to be that very eloquent accompanist.
sentences.s742-s58: He alighted from the bough of the dark cypress and went on with calmness and dignity.
sentences.s742-s59: He came and rubbed his face on the ground. Khosrow said to him, "Tell me, what sort of man are you?"
sentences.s742-s60: He said to him, "O King! I am your servant; by your voice I live in the world."
sentences.s742-s61: He recounted, thoroughly, whatever was in that bundle which entered that one heart and single body.
sentences.s742-s62: At seeing him the king grew joyful, like a rose-garden beneath the spring moon.
sentences.s742-s63: To the headstrong one he said thus: 'O ill-skilled fellow, you are like colocynth; Barbad is like sugar.'
sentences.s742-s64: Why did you keep him away from me? Were you reluctant to bring him into this assembly?
sentences.s742-s37: The king commanded those renowned men to search throughout that place of celebration.
sentences.s742-s38: They searched extensively and then returned; they came up close to the king.
sentences.s742-s39: The worldly‑wise man then took the floor (or took up the matter), saying that, given the king's fortune, this is not surprising.
sentences.s742-s40: May the green flower become his comforter; may his head and helmet be eternal.
sentences.s742-s29: From the iwan he came to that festive hall; the victor adorned the king's place.
sentences.s742-s30: A fair-faced cupbearer came; bearing a cup on her palm toward the king.
sentences.s742-s31: The sovereign withheld the child's wine; the crystal, reddened by the wine, became vanished.
sentences.s742-s32: At that moment when the sun turned pale, it remained so until the night became lapis‑lazuli (deep blue).
sentences.s742-s21: So that, when it was the king's festival, I might see one hidden aspect/face of the king.
sentences.s742-s22: The man said to him, 'What shall I do? I will take the thought out of your brain.'
sentences.s742-s23: When Khosrow was about to carry out his plot in the garden, the host's heart became like a bright lamp.
sentences.s742-s24: He went up to Barbad and said to him that the king intended to go to that festival.
sentences.s742-s9: He said to him, “It is right to be conciliatory, for he is superior to me in years and in skill.”
sentences.s742-s10: It must not happen before the king that we grow old and he be made new.
sentences.s742-s11: When the gatekeeper of the king heard of the rebellious man, he barred the way against the simple peacemaker.
sentences.s742-s12: When Barbad came near him, all his work was bad and his tune (or burden) was bad as well.
sentences.s742-s5: When the man heard that, greed boiled up in him, although he had no need of anything.
sentences.s742-s6: He left the country and went as far as the king's court; there he would watch the peacemakers.
sentences.s742-s7: When the rebellious one heard it his heart grew dark; at the song's wound he was transfixed.
sentences.s742-s8: He came to the lord's court, laid down a load of dirhams, and offered several dinars.
sentences.s742-s53: Thus he said that this creature was an angel, fashioned of musk and ambergris.
sentences.s742-s54: And if you had been a demon, you would not have sung a song; likewise you would not have known the lute's plucking.
sentences.s742-s55: Search in the garden to see where this is; the whole garden and flower-grove lie to the left and to the right.
sentences.s742-s56: I will fill mouth and face with jewels; upon this lute I will make its musicians chiefs.
sentences.s742-s65: At his voice she drew a joyful breath; she raised that very ruby cup and drank.
sentences.s742-s66: Thus, until she turned toward sleep, she filled her mouth with sweet drink.
sentences.s742-s67: It was Barbad, the master of comforters; one became famed among the chiefs.
sentences.s742-s68: Now the tale of Barbad has come to an end; may it not be that you have a bad companion.
sentences.s742-s41: When that king turned his face away from the beautiful one, the cupbearer brought another cup.
sentences.s742-s42: The player altered and tuned the instrument, and suddenly struck up another song.
sentences.s742-s43: which they used to sing in the turns of battle — from his voice they sent forth such a name.
sentences.s742-s44: When that learned man spoke and Khosrow heard, at his voice the king lifted the cup.
sentences.s742-s49: Once again the cry of the song rose up; that same artfully made voice of the instrument sounded.
sentences.s742-s50: You still sing 'green within green' now; in this manner they contrive deceit and enchantments.
sentences.s742-s51: When Parviz heard (this) he sprang to his feet; at his call he demanded a cup.
sentences.s742-s52: For in that cup there was one man (a measured amount) of wine; in a single instant he drained it.
sentences.s742-s45: He ordered, “Put this in its place; make the whole garden ready from top to bottom.”
sentences.s742-s46: They sprang in great numbers to every side of the garden and carried lamps beneath the trees.
sentences.s742-s47: They saw nothing but willow and cypress; stately, they scattered flowers beneath (their feet).
sentences.s742-s48: The king then asked for another cup; at the sound he straightaway raised his head.
sentences.s742-s13: You did not grant him the burden (office) of the commander; nor did you grant him what the people desired.
sentences.s742-s14: When he returned from that court in despair, he (with his lute) came toward the king's garden.
sentences.s742-s15: There was a gardener there named Marduy; on seeing him Barbad became joyful.
sentences.s742-s16: The king went to that garden for Nowruz; (Barbad) stayed there two weeks at that place of celebration.
sentences.s742-s17: Barbad went up among the men; that very day he became congenial with the man.
sentences.s742-s18: Thus Barbad spoke to the gardener: 'It is as if you are the soul and I the body.'
sentences.s742-s19: Now I will ask one thing of you: is there anywhere a little near you?
sentences.s742-s20: When the king of the world comes to this garden, give me a path so that I may see (him) secretly.
sentences.s742-s25: Barbad made all the garments green; likewise the barbat and the rud — (songs) of shame and of battle.
sentences.s742-s26: It came to such a point that you became king; sometimes you would sit in the spring, newly become.
sentences.s742-s27: A certain cypress, ill-green, and its leaves hungry; its branch like the back of a battlefield.
sentences.s742-s28: Upon that, the barbat came to stand beside the cypress; for a while they remained until the king (arrived).
sentences.s742-s33: The player took up his lute by that cypress, and that same one sang a song of heroism he had fashioned.
sentences.s742-s34: A certain nimble-handed fellow struck the tree, at which the ill-fated man stood amazed.
sentences.s742-s35: He raised an ode in a sweet voice, which you have now given and fashioned.
sentences.s742-s36: They remained in the assembly, amazed; each person then adopted a different opinion.
sentences.s742-s1: Whenever the sovereign passed by, his reign had reached its twenty-eighth year.
sentences.s742-s2: No one could commit an evil deed at his gate; Barbad learned of it from the royal court.
sentences.s742-s3: He said to him, 'Whoever the king of the world has chosen as a conciliator in secret...'
sentences.s742-s4: If he sets him equal to you, he will place upon your head the helmet over the rebellious (i.e., make you commander over the rebels).
